notes: Narrative source for the apostolic expansion from Jerusalem into the wider Roman world.

Early 3rd-century apocryphal acts (c. 220–240) composed in Syriac; narrates the apostle Thomas's journey to India; includes the Hymn of the Pearl and the Hymn of the Bride; emphasizes baptismal anointing, encratite continence, and eucharist; reflects eastern Syrian liturgical practice.
Theophilus of Antioch's three-book apology c. 180 defending Christian monotheism; first Christian use of the word Τριάς (Trinity); key witness for Logos theology and bodily resurrection.

Cyprian's earliest work c. 246, a personal letter to his friend Donatus describing his conversion and baptism; key witness for baptismal regeneration, the transformation of the Holy Spirit, and Cyprian's pre-episcopal spiritual formation.

Gregory Thaumaturgus's farewell speech to Origen delivered c. 238 upon leaving Caesarea; describes Origen's teaching method, curriculum, and pastoral approach; key primary source for Origen's pedagogical practice and the Caesarean school.

Tertullian's anti-Jewish treatise (c. 198–208); argues that Christ's dominion has reached nations beyond Roman access including Britannia; contains an early rhetorical claim of universal Christian spread.

Tertullian's five-book refutation of Marcion c. 207-208; defends the OT, the unity of the Creator and Redeemer, the reality of Christ's flesh, and bodily resurrection; the most comprehensive early anti-Marcionite polemic.
Tertullian's treatise against the modalist Praxeas c. 213; first systematic use of trinitas and tres personae una substantia; foundational document for Latin trinitarian vocabulary.

title_original: Ἔλεγχος καὶ ἀνατροπὴ τῆς ψευδωνύμου γνώσεως
notes: Irenaeus of Lyons's five-book refutation of Gnosticism c. 180, establishing apostolic succession, rule of faith, and the fourfold Gospel as the standard against Valentinian and Marcionite innovations.
Roman historical work by Tacitus used here for Nero and the fire/persecution context in Rome.
Josephus used as a near-ancient contextual witness.

Tertullian's defense of Christianity addressed to Roman magistrates c. 197; argues for freedom of religion and the innocence of Christians; source of the famous semen est sanguis Christianorum (the blood of Christians is seed).
Apology addressed to Hadrian or Antoninus Pius c. 125-130, defending Christians and describing their community life and practices.

Church order attributed to Hippolytus of Rome c. 215; contains the earliest known ordination rite for bishops, presbyters, and deacons; detailed baptismal liturgy including catechumenate, triple immersion, anointing, and eucharist; the earliest surviving eucharistic prayer (anaphora); regulations for church life including fasting, prayer hours, and care of the sick.

title_display: Book of the Laws of Countries
notes: Dialogue attributed to Bardaisan of Edessa (c. 196); defends human free will against astrological determinism; argues that diverse human customs across nations disprove fate; the earliest surviving Syriac literary composition.

Hippolytus's commentary on Daniel c. 204; the earliest known patristic biblical commentary to survive in substantial form; contains the earliest reference to Christ's birth being celebrated on December 25; interprets Daniel's prophecies christologically; opposes imminent eschatological expectations.

Ephrem the Syrian's verse-by-verse commentary on Tatian's Diatessaron (c. 360–370); the primary witness to how the Syriac church read the gospel harmony; christological and typological exegesis throughout.

Origen's massive commentary on the Gospel of John begun in Alexandria c. 226 and continued in Caesarea through c. 235; 32 books of which 9 survive substantially; foundational for Logos theology, eternal generation of the Son, and allegorical exegesis; includes polemics against Heracleon's Valentinian commentary on John.

Origen's commentary on Matthew composed in Caesarea c. 246–248; survives partially in Greek (Books 10–17) and in Latin translation; contains important eucharistic theology, ecclesiology, and discussion of church discipline; interprets Matthew 16:18 on Peter and the rock.

Origen's 15-book commentary on Romans composed in Caesarea c. 243–244; survives mostly in Rufinus's Latin translation; contains the most explicit early statement that infant baptism is an apostolic tradition received from the apostles; covers justification, free will, and the relation of Law and Gospel.
Qumran sectarian rule text often designated 1QS.

Origen's eight-book refutation of the pagan philosopher Celsus's True Doctrine, composed c. 248; the most substantial early Christian apologetic work; defends the incarnation, virgin birth, resurrection, free will, Christian ethics, and the superiority of Christian revelation over Greek philosophy; preserves extensive fragments of Celsus's lost attack.

Hippolytus's treatise against the modalist Noetus of Smyrna, c. 200–210; defends the distinction of Father and Son against Patripassianism; affirms the Logos as a distinct divine person who became incarnate; important pre-Nicene trinitarian witness.
Second Temple sectarian admonition and rule text associated with the Yahad tradition.
Tertullian's treatise on baptism c. 198-200; earliest surviving monograph on baptism; describes trinitarian formula, sponsors, and recommends delaying baptism of children.
Tertullian's treatise c. 206-212 defending the reality of Christ's incarnation against docetists and Marcion; argues that Christ took real human flesh from the virgin Mary.
Tertullian's treatise c. 211 on whether a Christian soldier may wear a military crown; argues that military service is incompatible with Christian faith due to idolatry and bloodshed.

title_display: De Dominica Oratione (On the Lord's Prayer)
notes: Cyprian's treatise on the Lord's Prayer c. 252; systematic exposition of each petition; emphasizes unity, the Eucharist as daily bread, forgiveness, and the communal nature of Christian prayer; heavily influenced by Tertullian's De Oratione but adds distinctly ecclesiological and eucharistic dimensions.

title_display: De Habitu Virginum (On the Dress of Virgins)
notes: Cyprian's treatise on virginity and ascetic discipline c. 249; his earliest treatise after conversion; exhorts consecrated virgins to modesty and spiritual discipline; key witness for the honor of virginity in the pre-Nicene North African church.

Cyprian's treatise c. 251 on the readmission of Christians who lapsed during the Decian persecution; distinguishes categories of lapse (sacrificati, thurificati, libellatici); insists on proper penance under episcopal authority before reconciliation; opposes both the rigorist denial of readmission (Novatian) and the lax granting of peace by confessors without episcopal sanction.
Tertullian's treatise on prayer c. 198-200; earliest known systematic exposition of the Lord's Prayer; includes eucharistic and fasting references.
Tertullian's early treatise on repentance c. 198-200; allows one post-baptismal repentance through church discipline; important early witness for penitential practice.
Tertullian's treatise c. 200 arguing that heretics have no right to appeal to Scripture; the Church alone possesses the apostolic tradition and succession that guarantees correct interpretation.

Origen's systematic theological work composed in Alexandria c. 220–230; the first attempt at a comprehensive Christian theology; four books covering God and the Trinity, the created world, free will, and Scripture; source for the doctrines of eternal generation of the Son, pre-existence of souls, universal restoration (apokatastasis), and allegorical exegesis; preserved mostly through Rufinus's sanitized Latin translation and fragments in Justinian's condemnation.
Tertullian's treatise c. 206-212 defending the literal bodily resurrection of the flesh against Gnostic spiritualizers.
Tertullian's treatise c. 197-202 arguing that Christians must abstain from public games, theater, and gladiatorial combat as incompatible with their baptismal vow.

Novatian's treatise on the Trinity c. 250; the first systematic Latin work on trinitarian theology; affirms the full divinity of Father, Son, and Holy Spirit; defends Christ's true incarnation and real suffering; orthodox in doctrine despite its author's later schism.

title_display: De Ecclesiae Catholicae Unitate (On the Unity of the Church)
notes: Cyprian's most influential treatise c. 251; argues that the unity of the Church is grounded in the unity of the episcopate; the see of Peter is the source and origin of episcopal unity; each bishop holds the whole of the one episcopate in solidum; schism and heresy are worse than apostasy because they rend the body of Christ; foundational text for Catholic ecclesiology.

Collection of 23 homilies by Aphrahat the Persian Sage; first ten (337) on Christian life and church order; next twelve (344) on persecution, Judaism, and eschatology; final demonstration (c. 345) on the grape; the earliest substantial corpus of Syriac Christian theology.

Justin Martyr's major apologetic dialogue c. 155-160 with the Jew Trypho; argues for OT fulfillment in Christ; identifies the Eucharist as the fulfillment of Malachi's pure offering; defends the virgin birth against Jewish objection.

Tatian the Assyrian's gospel harmony (c. 170) weaving the four canonical gospels into a single continuous narrative; the standard gospel text of the Syriac-speaking church until the 5th century; known through Ephrem's commentary and later witnesses.

Church order composed c. 230 in Syria; survives complete only in Syriac translation; covers bishops' authority, deacons, deaconesses, penance, baptismal anointing, fasting, eucharist, and catechumenate; later incorporated into the first six books of the Apostolic Constitutions.
Late antique church history used here for explicit memory of Peter and Paul suffering in Rome under Nero.
Apology of Athenagoras c. 177 addressed to Marcus Aurelius and Commodus, defending Christian monotheism, Logos theology, and ethics.
Early Christian letter of uncertain authorship c. 70-130 with extensive baptismal typology and anti-Jewish exegesis.
General epistle associated with James and notable for its moral exhortation.
Short general epistle associated with Jude and exhorting believers to contend for the faith.

title_display: Epistle of Polycarp to the Philippians
notes: Letter of Polycarp of Smyrna to the church at Philippi c. 110-135, warning against heresy and preserving apostolic tradition.
Personal Pauline letter concerning Onesimus and a household church network.
Pauline letter addressed to believers in Colossae.
Pauline letter traditionally associated with Ephesus and the Pauline imprisonment period.
Pauline letter central to disputes over Gentile inclusion and circumcision.
Homiletic epistle traditionally transmitted within the New Testament corpus.
Pauline letter addressed to the believers in Philippi.
Pauline letter addressed to the believers in Rome.
Pastoral epistle associated with Titus and the churches in Crete.
Seven epistles written by Ignatius of Antioch en route to martyrdom in Rome c. 110.

title_display: Epistula 64 (Letter to Fidus on Infant Baptism)
notes: Cyprian's letter c. 252 to Bishop Fidus in response to the question of whether baptism should be delayed until the eighth day after birth (by analogy with circumcision); the Council of Carthage 252 unanimously ruled that baptism should not be delayed — infants may be baptized as early as the second or third day; key witness for the assumption of infant baptism as standard practice and the council's reasoning that God's grace should not be withheld from any soul.

Cyprian's letter c. 256 to Bishop Jubaianus defending the rebaptism of heretics; argues that there is no baptism outside the Church because the Holy Spirit is absent from heretical communities; the one baptism belongs only to the one Church; extensively cited at the Council of Carthage 256; opposed by Pope Stephen I who held that heretical baptism in trinitarian form was valid.

Origen's treatise addressed to his patron Ambrose and the presbyter Protoctetus c. 235 during the persecution under Maximinus Thrax; exhorts them to embrace martyrdom rather than deny the faith; affirms free will, the vanity of idolatry, and the eschatological reward of the martyrs.
Justin Martyr defense of Christian faith addressed to Emperor Antoninus Pius c. 155.
Composite apocalyptic work influential in Second Temple Judaism and echoed in Jude.
Late first-century Roman Christian letter used here as an early witness to Peter and Paul as exemplary sufferers.
Johannine epistle emphasizing confession of Jesus as the Christ and the Son of God.
General epistle addressed to believers across several provinces of Asia Minor.
Pauline letter addressing the church in Corinth and preserving early resurrection tradition.
Pauline letter addressed to the church in Thessalonica.
Pastoral epistle associated with Timothy and Pauline instruction regarding Ephesus.
Textual witness for ministry-period claims.
Textual witness for ministry-period claims.
Included as canonical work identity because the seed dataset cites Mark as witness to ministry-period events.
Textual witness for ministry-period claims.

Origen's 20 surviving homilies on Jeremiah delivered in Caesarea c. 238–244; the only Origenian homiletical series surviving complete in Greek; rich in allegorical exegesis, penance theology, and the prophetic critique of external religion without inner conversion.

Origen's 16 homilies on Leviticus delivered in Caesarea c. 238–244; surviving in Rufinus's Latin translation; key source for Origen's eucharistic and sacrificial theology, baptismal purification, priestly typology, and penance; extensively allegorizes the Levitical rites as types of Christ and the church.

Origen's 39 homilies on Luke surviving in Jerome's Latin translation, delivered in Caesarea c. 233–234; key source for Origen's Mariology (perpetual virginity of Mary), infant baptism, and allegorical exegesis of the infancy and ministry narratives.
Melito of Sardis's homily c. 160-170, earliest extant Christian homily, presenting Christ as the Passover lamb and pre-existent Lord incarnate.
Ephrem the Syrian's polemical hymn cycle (c. 363–373) refuting Bardaisan, Marcion, and Mani; defends orthodox christology, creation, and free will against deterministic and dualistic systems.

Ephrem the Syrian's hymn cycle on faith (c. 363–373); explores the Trinity, incarnation, baptism, and eucharist through paradox and symbol; composed at Edessa after the fall of Nisibis; a cornerstone of Syriac Nicene theology.
Ephrem the Syrian's hymn cycle on Christ's birth (c. 363–373); rich Marian theology, incarnation paradoxes, and typological connections between Eve and Mary, Eden and Bethlehem.
Ephrem the Syrian's 15-hymn cycle on Paradise (c. 363–373); cosmic typology connecting Eden, the Church, and eschatological restoration; symbolic theology of mountain, tree, and garment.
Retelling of Genesis and Exodus with calendrical and covenantal emphases.
Anonymous apology c. 130-200 addressed to a pagan inquirer describing Christians as a third race living in the world but not of it; contains significant Logos theology.

Lost work by Marcion of Sinope, c. 140–144 AD. A systematic presentation of contradictions between the God of the Old Testament (just, wrathful) and the God revealed by Jesus (good, merciful). Foundational document of Marcionite theology. Known through refutations by Irenaeus (AH 1.27) and Tertullian (Against Marcion).

Marcion's edited version of the Gospel of Luke, c. 140–144 AD; the first written Christian canon. Marcion removed all references to the Jewish God, genealogies, birth narratives, and OT citations. Known through Irenaeus AH 1.27.2, 3.12 and Tertullian's Against Marcion Book 4.
Earliest Christian martyrdom acta from the church at Smyrna c. 155-156 recording Polycarp's death.

Hippolytus's treatise c. 200 on eschatology and the Antichrist; one of the earliest surviving complete patristic works on the subject; interprets Daniel and Revelation; affirms bodily resurrection and final judgment; opposes imminent apocalypticism.

Origen's treatise on prayer composed in Caesarea c. 233–234; discusses the nature, necessity, and theology of prayer; defends free will against determinism; contains a detailed exegesis of the Lord's Prayer; affirms the Trinity and prayer addressed to the Father through the Son.

title_display: On the Resurrection of the Dead
notes: Athenagoras's philosophical defense of bodily resurrection c. 177.
Tatian the Assyrian's polemical address c. 155-165 against Greco-Roman religion and philosophy; defends Christian resurrection hope and Logos theology; student of Justin Martyr.

Clement of Alexandria's three-book moral formation work c. 198 AD, guiding newly baptized Christians in ascetic discipline, sacramental life, and ethical formation; rich source for baptismal illumination, eucharist, fasting, marriage, and avoidance of public spectacles.

Papias of Hierapolis's five-book exposition c. 95-110 preserved in fragments by Eusebius and Irenaeus; key witness for oral apostolic tradition, teacher succession, Matthew's logia, and millenarianism.

Arian church historian Philostorgius's 12-book history (c. 425) covering 300–425; survives mostly in Photius's epitome; key source for Theophilus the Indian's mission to Himyar (3.4–6) and Constantius II's religious diplomacy.
Clement of Alexandria's evangelistic address to pagans c. 195 AD; presents Christ as the new song and divine Logos calling all humanity to conversion and baptism.
Collection of Jewish psalms with strong eschatological and Davidic expectations.

Clement of Alexandria's homily c. 195–215 AD on Mark 10:17–31 (the rich young ruler). Argues that wealth is not intrinsically evil but must be used through almsgiving as stewards of God's gifts; includes the story of John restoring a lapsed youth (ch. 42), supporting post-baptismal repentance.

title_original: Φιλοσοφούμενα ἢ κατὰ πασῶν αἱρέσεων ἔλεγχος
notes: Major heresiological work attributed to Hippolytus c. 222–225 (also known as Philosophoumena); ten books refuting pagan philosophies and Christian heresies; Books 9–10 provide unique information about Roman church disputes under Zephyrinus and Callistus; affirms Logos theology against modalism.
Apocalyptic work associated with Patmos and the late first-century church.

Rufinus of Aquileia's continuation of Eusebius's Church History in two books (HE 10–11, c. 402); primary Western source for Frumentius and the christianization of Aksum (1.9); covers events from Nicaea to Theodosius I.
Short Johannine epistle addressed to the elect lady and her children.
General epistle associated with Peter and warning against false teachers.
Pauline letter addressed to the church in Corinth together with believers in Achaia.
Pauline letter addressed to the church in Thessalonica.
Pastoral epistle associated with Timothy and Paul's later imprisonment setting.

Minutes of the Council of Carthage held September 1, 256 under Cyprian's presidency; 87 bishops from North Africa each individually voted that heretical baptism is invalid and converts must be rebaptized; the fullest extant record of North African episcopal collegial practice; each bishop's sententium preserved verbatim; opposed by Pope Stephen I and eventually overturned by later church practice accepting heretical baptism.
Vision and parable collection from the Roman church c. 100-150 emphasizing post-baptismal penance and the possibility of one second repentance.
Wisdom work emphasizing repentance, almsgiving, and practical piety.
Socrates Scholasticus's church history (c. 439) covering 305–439; independent confirmation of the Frumentius–Aksum story (HE 1.19); important Eastern source for post-Nicene developments.

Clement of Alexandria's eight-book synthesis c. 198–203 AD of Christian faith and Greek philosophy; key source for apostolic tradition, rule of faith, succession, virginity, continual prayer, and philosophy as a preparatory schoolmaster for the gospel.
Early church order manual dated c. 70-120, containing baptismal and eucharistic instructions.
Short Johannine epistle addressed to Gaius.

Eusebius of Caesarea's panegyrical biography of Emperor Constantine I (c. 337–339); four books covering Constantine's reign and religious policy; includes the letter of Constantius II to Ezana regarding Frumentius.
Qumran eschatological battle text often designated 1QM.
Jewish wisdom text from the late Second Temple period.
